title: When You Feel Like the Villain
domain: Moral injury, guilt, shame, and spirituality
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: Field notes for when you believe you're the perpetrator, not the victim.

content.text: You see yourself as the bad guy. Maybe you hurt someone (physically, emotionally, professionally). Maybe you were ordered to do something and you did it. Maybe you froze when someone needed help. Maybe you didn't speak up. Now you believe you're the villain in this story. You're the threat. You're the perpetrator. This isn't guilt about one action—it's an identity conviction: you ARE the problem, and that's permanent and unforgivable. The shame is different from regular guilt. It's character-level condemnation.

items: Self-as-villain identity: You see yourself as fundamentally bad, not just having done something bad.
Inability to forgive yourself: You can believe others deserve compassion, but not you.
Rumination on harmful actions: You replay what you did, collecting evidence that you're evil.
Self-punishment: You engage in self-harm, self-sabotage, or self-imposed isolation.
Difficulty accepting compliments: If someone says you're good, you dismiss it or feel they're confused.
Hypervigilance to your own badness: You're watching for evidence that proves you're the perpetrator.
Avoidance of victims or those you harmed: You can't face them because they'll confirm you're evil.
Belief in your own irredeemability: You've done something unforgivable so repair is impossible.

lines: This is shame, not character diagnosis:
Believing you're fundamentally bad is a trauma response, not a fact about your worth.

content.text: You did something (or failed to do something) and your nervous system concluded 'I am the threat.' If what you did hurt someone, shame locked in. If you were ordered to do something harmful, and you obeyed, shame tells you that obedience proves you ARE willing to cause harm. Your brain conflates action with identity. You protect yourself with more self-punishment (if you hurt yourself before others can, you have control). Add moral injury (you violated your own conscience) and you get a locked belief: I am bad, unforgivable, and irredeemable.

items: Rumination loops: Action → shame → collecting evidence of badness → deeper conviction of being villain.
Self-punishment escalating: Minor mistakes become proof of evil intent.
Isolation deepening: You avoid people who might love you and prove the shame wrong.
Hypervigilance to your own capacity for harm: Every normal impulse (frustration, self-interest) is evidence you're bad.
Belief in permanence: 'I did X, therefore I am bad forever. This never changes.'
Refusal of repair: Even if someone forgives you, you can't accept it—it doesn't match your self-image.
Secondary shame: Shame about the shame. You know other people don't beat themselves this way, so you're weak too.

lines: Why this works: reducing total load and increasing predictability lowers baseline arousal and improves regulation.

content.text: You can rebuild a more complex relationship with what you did—separating the action from your worth, and understanding context without excusing the harm.

items: Name the action specifically: What did you do or fail to do? Not 'I'm a bad person,' but 'I did X.'
Understand the context: Were you ordered to do it? Were you in survival mode? Were you the only one choosing? Context is not excuse, it's information.
Separate action from identity: You did something wrong. That makes you human, not evil. Humans make mistakes, cause harm, or fail under pressure.
Examine the rumination: Your brain is collecting evidence of your badness. Notice it's selective. Would you judge someone else this harshly? Why is your standard different?
Make specific amends if possible AND safe: Direct apology, changed behavior, restitution—something concrete if the person wants it.
Accept that amends might not be accepted: Even if you repair, the person may need to stay away. That's their right and it doesn't mean you're still evil.
Practice self-compassion: If a person you love did what you did in the same context, could you see their humanity? Can you apply that same seeing to yourself?
Address the specific moral injury: Work with someone on the gap between your actions and your values.
Rebuild your identity carefully: You're not good or bad. You're complex. You're capable of harm and repair. Both are true.

lines: Field rule: set the bar low enough to do it even on your worst day.

content.text: Stress communication should be clear, brief, and repeatable. Predictable routines protect relationships.

items: Use concrete language: 'I did something I regret deeply. I'm trying to understand how it happened and how to live with it.'
Name what you need: 'I need help believing I'm not fundamentally evil. I need to know repair is possible.'
Invite them in: 'I'm struggling with guilt and shame about what I did. I need your patience while I figure this out.'
Support their reaction: If they're hurt, they get to be. You don't get to demand forgiveness to feel better about yourself.

content.text: Self-guided tools are a start. Escalate care when symptoms persist, spread, or begin harming safety and function.

items: Self-harm ideation or behavior is accompanying the shame.
You're unable to accept forgiveness or make amends because you believe you're irredeemable.
The shame is paralyzing your functioning at work or home.
You're isolating because you believe no one would love you if they knew.
Trauma-informed therapy or groups for moral injury specifically address this self-villain identity and help rebuild self-compassion.

content.text: Use the worksheet every day for one week. Track trigger, body signal, and reset action. Keep what works and remove what does not.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: When You Feel Like the Villain
worksheets.intro: Use this to track the pattern in this chapter and build a practical response you can repeat under load.

question_groups.label: Where did this pattern show up this week?
question_groups.hint: List specific moments at work, at home, and in transition.

question_groups.label: What did it cost you when it took over?
question_groups.hint: Name impact on body, mood, decisions, and relationships.

question_groups.label: What two actions will you run for the next 7 days?
question_groups.hint: Choose actions small enough to use on hard days.

numbered_prompts.hint: What did your body do first?
What exact behavior will you use next time?

worksheets.closing_note: Track this daily for one week, then tighten the plan around what actually worked.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: When You Feel Like the Villain - Partner/Team Check-In
worksheets.intro: Use this page for one short practical check-in each week.

question_groups.label: What do others notice first when this pattern is active?
What support helps most and least?
question_groups.hint: List what helps and what escalates the issue.

question_groups.label: What agreement will you both practice this week?
question_groups.hint: One clear action from each side.

numbered_prompts.hint: What phrase means pause and reset?
What specific repair happens after a rupture?

worksheets.closing_note: Keep check-ins short, specific, and repeatable.
